national_expectations.summary: As co-hosts reaching the knockout stage for the first time, Canada are favourites against South Africa and carry real belief; before the tournament Jesse Marsch had openly set winning the group as the target.

national_mood.summary: Optimism tinged with frustration: a final-day loss to Switzerland cost Canada top spot and home advantage, sending them to Los Angeles rather than Vancouver, but the mood has lifted with Alphonso Davies fit to return for the knockouts.

manager_situation.summary: Jesse Marsch is backed and ambitious but publicly rued losing home advantage, even admitting a tactical regret from the Switzerland defeat; he confirmed Davies would be ready for the next match.

historical_context.summary: This is Canada's first-ever World Cup knockout match, and a first competitive meeting with South Africa — the only prior encounter was a friendly that South Africa won.

dominant_storylines.storyline: Alphonso Davies returns from a hamstring injury for the Round of 32 — a major lift after he was held back through the group stage.
Canada's attacking threat — led by Jonathan David and Cyle Larin — against a disciplined South African defence is the tactical sub-plot.
Midfielder Ismael Kone is out for the rest of the tournament with a broken leg, a blow to Canada's transition play.

The 'decoy' controversy: Canadian outlets report Marsch openly defended keeping Alphonso Davies off the field against Switzerland and using him as a 'leurre' (decoy) to make opponents believe he could come on, and he refused to apologize for chasing any competitive edge at a World Cup.

A 'will-he-or-won't-he' fog around Davies: Marsch declared the captain 'available to play' but pointedly refused to confirm a start or minutes, which national columnists framed as a light cloud of anxiety over Canada's most important player on the eve of a first knockout match.

Davies' own account of being benched: in the national press the captain revealed he went to Marsch before the Switzerland match asking to play a few minutes and was sat down and reminded he still wasn't ready — a hard message he later accepted was correct.

An aggressive-approach pledge from the dressing room: with the Canadian press expecting a fast, spirited South Africa side after its group-stage upset of South Korea, winger Tajon Buchanan said the priority in Los Angeles is to be aggressive and confident and to play Canada's own style.

'No underestimating' South Africa: Canadian players publicly warned against complacency, pointing to South Africa's surprise win over South Korea, while defender Alistair Johnston read it as an open, transition-heavy game between two sides that like to run.

A 'last chance for Davies the icon' framing: a Globe and Mail columnist argued this knockout run may be a closing window for the 25-year-old to cement himself as a Canadian sporting icon, with his international opportunities growing more limited and four years until the next World Cup.

South African provocation noted in Canada: the Canadian press flagged that South Africa's sports minister Gayton McKenzie publicly predicted a 3-0 win for his side, adding needle to a first knockout meeting between the two nations.

attributed_quotes.quote: I'm disappointed we weren't able to get a win or draw to keep us here.
I'm not confirming any minutes, starts, subs. He's available to play tomorrow.
attributed_quotes.context: reported; Marsch on Alphonso Davies' availability vs South Africa

attributed_quotes.quote: He could have said yes, but he sat me down and reminded me I wasn't ready yet. I thought about it and it's true he was right.
attributed_quotes.quote_original: Il aurait pu me dire oui, mais il m'a assis et m'a rappelé que je n'étais pas encore prêt. J'y ai pensé et c'est vrai qu'il avait raison.
attributed_quotes.context: reported; Davies on asking Marsch to play vs Switzerland and being told no

attributed_quotes.quote: It's about being aggressive, playing our style of play. The most important thing is being aggressive.
attributed_quotes.context: reported; Buchanan on Canada's approach vs South Africa

gaps: No official Canada Soccer statement sourced; quotes are via media reporting (Marsch's remarks relayed by ESPN from TSN).
Opponent-specific (South Africa) preparation detail not researched here — see RSA.json.
Davies' starting role vs minutes off the bench not confirmed beyond 'fit to return'.
